name: Ana Progovac
title: Assistant Professor
summary: I joined NYU Grossman School of Medicine's Institute for Excellence in Health Equity and Department of Population Health in Fall 2024. I was previously an Assistant Professor in Psychiatry at Harvard Medical School (HMS) and Senior Scientist at Cambridge Health Alliance (CHA) Health Equity Research Lab, where I had been collaborating with clinicians and other researchers since 2015 when I began my NIMH-sponsored postdoctoral training at Harvard Medical School. My research combines mixed-methods, implementation science, pragmatic, and community participatory research methods to study mental health for historically underserved U.S. populations. I have received grants as Principal Investigator from NIMHD and Harvard Medical School to support my work using implementation science research methods and frameworks to improve equity and patient-centeredness in mental health care, including for patients from diverse racial/ethnic, language, and sexual orientation and gender identity backgrounds. I am also a 2023-2025 Implementation Research Institute (IRI) Fellow supported by NIMH.

name: Rajesh Vedanthan
title: Associate Professor
summary: My academic and professional journey has consistently been an exploration of how to bring together different academic disciplines to improve understanding of human behavior, our interactions with each other, our interactions with the planet, and implications for global health. After completing a double-major in economics and chemistry at Swarthmore College, I was fortunate to be selected as a Marshall Scholar and studied Human Sciences at the University of Oxford. For my thesis, I combined genetics, evolutionary biology, demography, urban geography, sociology, and anthropology to develop a cohesive understanding of women’s health-seeking behavior in southern India. For medical school, I enrolled in the University of California-Berkeley/San Francisco Joint Medical Program, during which time I synthesized perspectives and insights from epidemiology, clinical medicine, critical theory, gender studies, and medical anthropology to examine the relationship between domestic violence and HIV risk in southern India.  Currently, I am a physician-scientist who is internationally recognized for my contributions to improving global cardiovascular health and health equity. My research is purposely transdisciplinary—while my main fields of expertise are clinical cardiology, epidemiology, and implementation research, I have consciously and purposefully pursued a research agenda that combines insights and approaches from a diversity of academic disciplines, including anthropology, economics, network analysis, systems science, syndemic analysis, environmental health, climate science, operations research, design thinking, and data science.  I have been continuously supported by NIH grants for over a decade and have established a global cardiovascular health implementation research portfolio to develop, test, and evaluate innovative solutions to address implementation gaps in delivering evidence-based care in several parts of the world. Our implementation approaches have combined innovations in health workforce strategies, health information technology, and directly addressing social determinants of health such as poverty.   In addition to my research and clinical activities, I am deeply committed to capacity building in both the clinical and research arenas. Over the past decade, I have mentored several dozen trainees, leading to several abstracts, publications, and research awards.   I have been recognized for this work by several domestic and international organizations, including the American College of Cardiology, American Heart Association, National Institutes of Health, and the Global Alliance for Chronic Diseases. I am an editorial board member of several cardiovascular and global health journals.

publications.title: Spatial environmental factors predict cardiovascular and all-cause mortality: Results of the SPACE study
publications.authors: Hadley MB; Nalini M; Adhikari S; Szymonifka J; et al; Vedanthan R
publications.plain_language_summary: In a large cohort in a lower-resource region, multiple place-based exposures including PM2.5, household fuel and ventilation, proximity to major roads, and distance to PCI centers independently predicted cardiovascular and all-cause mortality, underscoring environmental and access interventions as high-yield targets.
key_findings: Ambient PM2.5 was associated with higher all-cause mortality (HR about 1.20 per μg/m3 in the model) and higher cardiovascular mortality signal.
Household kerosene use without a chimney and greater distance to PCI centers were associated with higher cardiovascular mortality.
Population attributable fractions suggested a substantial mortality burden attributable to modifiable environmental exposures.
publications.equity_relevance: Links structural and environmental exposures to mortality in a low-income setting, supporting policy and implementation approaches for place-based prevention.

publications.title: Hypertension management in rural western Kenya: a needs-based health workforce estimation model
publications.authors: Vedanthan R; Lee DJ; Kamano JH; et al
publications.source_file: Rajesh_Vedanthan-HTN Workforce Estimation Model Hum Res Health 2019.pdf
publications.plain_language_summary: A practical, adjustable Excel-based model estimates full-time equivalent staffing needs for nurse-led hypertension care in rural Kenya using local protocols plus empirical time-motion and stakeholder input, enabling scale-up planning.
key_findings: Model outputs vary by enrollment scenario; illustrative ramp-up scenario projected large increases in nurse FTE needs by year 3.
Framework explicitly separates clinical encounter demand from staffing capacity and can be tuned to local assumptions.
publications.equity_relevance: Directly supports scalable hypertension control strategies where clinician shortages and access barriers drive inequities.

publications.title: Community-based medication delivery program for antihypertensive medications improves adherence and reduces blood pressure
publications.authors: Tran DN; Kangogo K; Amisi JA; et al; Vedanthan R; Pastakia SD
publications.source_file: Rajesh_Vedanthan-Community-based medication delivery HTN PLOS One 2022.pdf
publications.plain_language_summary: A pilot community medication delivery model coupled medication drop-offs with BP checks and adherence assessment, improving medication possession and adherence and showing larger SBP reductions with more deliveries.
key_findings: More successful deliveries were associated with better medication possession and improved self-reported adherence.
Observed SBP reductions were larger among patients receiving two or more deliveries than among those receiving none.
publications.equity_relevance: Targets structural barriers such as transportation cost and pharmacy access that disproportionately affect low-income patients.

publications.title: Protecting Cardiovascular Health From Wildfire Smoke
publications.authors: Hadley MB; Henderson SB; Brauer M; Vedanthan R
publications.plain_language_summary: A clinical and health-system roadmap for reducing cardiovascular harms from wildfire smoke, emphasizing exposure reduction, clean indoor air, risk stratification, and proactive management of cardiovascular risk factors.
key_findings: Summarizes evidence linking short-term wildfire smoke exposure to cardiovascular mortality, hospitalization, acute coronary syndrome, and stroke.
Outlines actionable interventions including indoor air filtration, clean-air shelters, respirators, and health-system planning.
publications.equity_relevance: Highlights disproportionate exposure and vulnerability among disadvantaged groups and provides implementable mitigation strategies.

publications.title: Group Medical Visit and Microfinance Intervention for Patients With Diabetes or Hypertension in Kenya
publications.authors: Vedanthan R; Kamano JH; Chrysanthopoulou SA; et al; Fuster V
publications.journal: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
publications.source_file: Rajesh_Vedanthan- Group Medical Visit and Microfinance.pdf
publications.plain_language_summary: A large cluster trial tested microfinance and group medical visits, alone and combined, showing clinically meaningful SBP reductions with signals of greater benefit in women and lower-wealth participants when social and clinical supports were integrated.
key_findings: SBP declined in all arms; the combined GMV plus microfinance arm showed the largest mean SBP reduction, with confidence intervals consistent with benefit to neutral effect relative to usual care.
Subgroup patterns suggested stronger effects for women and for participants with lower socioeconomic indicators.
publications.equity_relevance: Explicitly integrates economic stability and social support into chronic disease care delivery to reduce access-driven disparities.

name: Antoinette Schoenthaler
title: Professor
summary: I have a profound interest in understanding the mechanisms underlying racial disparities in cardiovascular health. The programmatic focus of my research is the translation of evidence-based interventions to clinical care to systematically address the fundamental barriers to medication adherence in diverse patients with cardiovascular diseases. I have established a rigorous research program focused on: a) identifying and intervening upon the key psychosocial and interpersonal determinants of medication adherence in patients with chronic diseases - especially among minority populations who suffer disproportionately higher burden of  cardiovascular diseases; and b) developing and implementing systems-level interventions that foster collaborative team-based models of care for improvement of medication adherence and disease control among patients with cardiovascular diseases. Over the past 11 years, I have been involved in multiple funded studies as Principal or Co-Principal Investigator, with an overall focus on patient- and provider-level interventions to address cardiovascular disease in practice (clinical)-based settings.  I also have extensive experience in use of motivational interviewing as a person-centered counseling technique.

name: Cristina Gonzalez
title: Professor
summary: Cristina M. Gonzalez, M.D., M.Ed., is Professor of Medicine and Population Health and Associate Director—Medical Education—Institute for Excellence in Health Equity at New York University Grossman School of Medicine. An alumna of Albert Einstein College of Medicine, she completed internal medicine residency at New York Presbyterian Hospital- Weill Cornell Medical Center, and medical education research fellowship at University of Cincinnati, earning a Master’s Degree in Medical Education. Dr. Gonzalez is a health equity medical education researcher who has been grant funded since 2010. Through both NIH and Foundation funding she has advanced the science in addressing the impact of implicit bias on clinical encounters and clinical learning environments. Her lab developed the patient-informed framework of Implicit Bias Recognition and Management (IBRM) and continues to design, implement, and evaluate interventions seeking to improve clinicians’ skills in IBRM.  Dr. Gonzalez is an internationally renowned expert in the development of skills-based, behavioral interventions in IBRM for physicians across the continuum of training and practice. Through NIH funding, her lab was the first to develop calibrated, high-fidelity simulations that precipitate the influence of racial implicit bias to study the efficacy of future IBRM interventions on physician communication skills and patient outcomes. Through funding from the National Academy of Medicine, her lab advanced the study of the diagnostic process to contribute to diagnostic equity. She is currently developing and AI-powered virtual coach in the room to provide feedback to physicians on communication skills related to racial implicit bias and developing interventions to support equitable clinical decision-making by dentists with ongoing NIH support.

name: Angela Aifah
title: Assistant Professor
summary: I am a social work–trained implementation scientist with over ten years of experience conducting research on integrated care in global settings. My work focuses on evidence-informed implementation science to address the dual burden of communicable and noncommunicable diseases in low-resource, high-need contexts, with an emphasis on community-engaged and partnership-driven approaches. My research centers on integrating services for comorbid conditions, building healthcare workforce capacity, engaging affected populations in intervention adaptation, and delivering contextually tailored implementation strategies. I have contributed to domestic and international research addressing HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, substance use, and cardiovascular disease. My work has been published in leading journals, including Implementation Science, Progress in Cardiovascular Diseases, and European Heart Journal. As an Assistant Professor in Global Health, I apply implementation science frameworks to scale evidence-based interventions for hypertension and HIV comorbidity, incorporating stakeholder engagement strategies such as human-centered design and differentiated service delivery models. I serve as Co-Investigator on the MAP-IT study in Akwa-Ibom, Nigeria, focused on integrated hypertension and HIV care, and am Principal Investigator of a K01 career development award (K01HL165086) to co-develop and evaluate a community–clinical linkage strategy to improve care for individuals living with HIV and hypertension in resource-constrained settings.

name: Tanya M. Spruill
title: Associate Professor
summary: Tanya Spruill, PhD, Associate Professor of Population Health and Medicine at the NYU Grossman School of Medicine, is a clinical psychologist and behavioral medicine researcher. She is a core faculty member in the NYU Langone Institute for Excellence in Health Equity where she leads the Mental Health Research Pillar. Dr. Spruill’s primary research interests concern the adverse health effects of chronic stress and depression. With funding from the NIH, CDC and American Heart Association (AHA), she is conducting observational studies of the impact of stress on chronic disease outcomes and clinical trials testing telehealth-delivered mindfulness interventions to reduce stress and depression in diverse populations including hypertension, cardiovascular disease and epilepsy. A major focus is adapting these interventions for women and minoritized groups to enhance their impact at the population level. Currently, Dr. Spruill is PI of a NIH-funded clinical trial testing effects of a Zoom-delivered mindfulness intervention on blood pressure and mental health outcomes in Black women with uncontrolled hypertension. She is also PI of clinical trials testing mindfulness-based interventions to improve mental health outcomes in patients with myocardial infarction and epilepsy, respectively. She is Co-Director of the Coordinating Center for the RESTORE Network, an AHA-funded health equity research network. RESTORE is conducting five hybrid effectiveness-implementation trials testing strategies that address social determinants of health to implement hypertension prevention interventions in Black communities. She is also MPI of the NHLBI-funded Jackson Heart Study Hypertension Working Group, which examines adverse social determinants of health, health behaviors, and other factors impacting hypertension risk and blood pressure control among Black adults. Dr. Spruill’s research to date has demonstrated that: (1) elevated stress and depressive symptoms are common chronic disease comorbidities and negatively impact health outcomes; (2) mindfulness-based interventions can reduce stress and depression in racially and ethnically diverse populations with chronic disease; and (3) telehealth methods can be leveraged to increase access to evidence-based psychosocial interventions. The overarching goal of her research is to develop and implement effective, scalable, culturally sensitive psychosocial interventions that promote mental health equity, improve quality of life, and support chronic disease prevention and management.

name: JohnRoss Rizzo
title: Professor
summary: : John-Ross (JR) Rizzo, MD, MSc, FACRM is a physician-scientist-leader at NYU Langone Health. Dr. Rizzo is the Health System Director of Universal Access and Vice Chair of Health Equity & Innovation at the Rusk Institute of Rehabilitation Medicine at the NYU Grossman School of Medicine. He also holds appointments in the departments of neurology, ophthalmology, mechanical and aerospace engineering, and biomedical engineering at the NYU Tandon School of Engineering. He heads the Visumotor Integration Laboratory, exploring motor control with a special emphasis on visual guidance and biomechanics in blindness, and the Rehabilitation Engineering Alliance and Center Transforming Low Vision Laboratory (REACTIV), exploring bio-inspired, multi-sensory assistive technologies with a special emphasis on advanced wearables. He is an advisor to multiple startups and early ventures focused broadly on technological disruption for those with visual impairments of all kinds and holds a series of board and advisory roles at leading NPOs, inclusive of the MTA, National Academies, Lighthouse Guild, FFB, CRF, City Access, and more. Dr. Rizzo capitalizes on his lived experience with vision loss in all aspects of his work.

name: Manasi Kumar
title: Faculty
summary: I am a Research Professor with strong strengthening health systems and policy training background cross-cutting areas around mental health and substance use treatment, prevention, policy development with a strong focus on vulnerable contexts and populations. I have over 15 years of experience in cross-cultural psychopathology and mental health and substance use interventions research in low resource contexts. More recently I am working with WHO’s Department of Mental Health/Brain Health and Substance Use on two areas: developing monitoring and evaluation frameworks to advance population mental health and substance use surveillance and developing community mental health programs in diverse geographies to address ‘treatment gap’.   I will bring to this study my 10 years of experience in carrying out mental health services strengthening and developing policy relevant messages from evidence based mental health services research in low resource contexts. Some data monitoring reports where I have been a scientific contributor include  a)	Guidance on mental health policy and strategic action plans. Geneva: World Health Organization (WHO); 2025 (Guidance on mental health policy and strategic action plans). b) Global report on children with developmental disabilities: from the margins to the mainstream. Geneva: World Health Organization and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF), 2023, c) WHO. Guide for integration of perinatal mental health in maternal and child health services. Geneva: World Health Organization; 2022, d) United Nations Children’s Fund, The State of the World’s Children 2021: On My Mind – Promoting, protecting and caring for children’s mental health, UNICEF, New York, October 2021. The proposed study ‘Integrating IEHE and DPH Data Hubs to advance understanding of comorbidities of Mental and Substance Use Disorders: Accelerating an embedded learning health ecosystem’ will help advance understanding of how mental health and substance use related surveillance and clinical management can be advanced within NYU health care systems and will provide IEHE with additional tools and evidence to integrate disparate datahubs to harmonize and learn from mental health and substance use related data sources that may exist within different hubs. It is also a pathfinder demonstration project that may help in improving understanding of the use, quality, workflows of existing screening tools and assessment processes leading to management of comorbid conditions like Substance use and mental disorders. It is a project also undertaken by a researcher with largely an LMIC and health equity focus to explore how disparities and health complexities can be enhanced within NYU care landscape using lessons from low resource settings.

The proposed study ‘Integrating IEHE and DPH Data Hubs to advance understanding of comorbidities of Mental and Substance Use Disorders: Accelerating an embedded learning health ecosystem’ will help advance understanding of how mental health and substance use related surveillance and clinical management can be advanced within NYU health care systems and will provide IEHE with additional tools and evidence to integrate disparate datahubs to harmonize and learn from mental health and substance use related data sources that may exist within different hubs. It is also a pathfinder demonstration project that may help in improving understanding of the use, quality, workflows of existing screening tools and assessment processes leading to management of comorbid conditions like Substance use and mental disorders. It is a project also undertaken by a researcher with largely an LMIC and health equity focus to explore how disparities and health complexities can be enhanced within NYU care landscape using lessons from low resource settings.
summary_short: I am a Research Professor with strong strengthening health systems and policy training background cross-cutting areas around mental health and substance use treatment, prevention, policy development with a strong focus on vulnerable contexts and populations. I have over 15 years of experience in cross-cultural psychopathology and mental health and substance use interventions research in low resource contexts.

name: Marie Brault
title: Assistant Professor
summary: I have the expertise and training necessary to be an MPI on this R01 application entitled, “A hybrid type 3 trial to assess implementation and effectiveness of group interpersonal psychotherapy for adolescent depression in school settings.” This project will use implementation mapping to adapt, select, and trial implementation strategies and conduct a hybrid type 3 trial of a triggerconcordant group interpersonal psychotherapy (IPT-G), with accompanying implementation strategies for youth aged 15-19 years old in Uganda. My background in biology and anthropology, as well as post-doctoral training in public health and implementation science have provided me with training in ethnographic and community-based participatory methodologies related to adolescent sexual and reproductive health, mental health, survey development/adaptation and quantitative methods, implementation research design and methodologies, and the role of multi-level factors in shaping health. I laid the groundwork to participate as MPI for the proposed research as a research assistant on NIH grants focused on multi-level interventions to address sexual and mental health, substance use and HIV in India, co-Principal Investigator on an NSF grant focused on youth health in India, behavioral scientist and qualitative methodologist on multi-sited projects concerning social determinants of health and health system interventions in the U.S. and Sub- Saharan Africa, co-investigator on research to improve adolescent demand for HIV and other sexual and reproductive health services in eSwatini, and PI on a K12 focused on the co-creation and implementation of youth-friendly practices in community and clinical settings to improve the sexual and reproductive health of young women in the U.S. and India. My role as Behavioral Scientist on a WHO-funded 4-country study of barriers and facilitators to under-five child survival, provided me with experience in the management of multi-sited qualitative research, including training data collection teams in four African countries (Kenya, Liberia, Zambia, and Zimbabwe), data management, analysis, and dissemination to both academic and Ministry of Health audiences. I am also a collaborator with the African Cohort Study (AFRICOS), a longitudinal study of youth and adults living with and without HIV across Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda. These experiences have allowed me to develop skills in managing multidisciplinary and multisited teams, in the areas of youth-friendly service provision, mixed methods implementation research, and rapid cycle human-centered design and research in community and clinical environments, as well as develop methods for recruiting adolescent participants and measuring relevant research domains. Since joining NYU, I have begun collaborating with MPI Kim and Co-Is Kasujja and Bershteyn on secondar analyses related to their trigger-concordant IPT-G trial with adults in Uganda. The proposed research builds on and extends my work in Sub-Saharan Africa, and my recent ended Fogarty-funded K01 to adapt and conduct a hybrid type 2 pilot trial of a community/clinic youth-friendly intervention to support the sexual, reproductive, and mental health of adolescent girls and young women in Mumbai, India. My prior work and expertise will allow me to work effectively with my co-MPIs Drs. Musanje and Kim, and co-Is Drs. Kasujja and Bershteyn and to adapt and trial implementation strategies for effective IPT-G delivery in schools.

name: Omonigho M. Bubu
title: Associate Professor
summary: I am the Director of the Aging Research in Sleep Equity & Dementia Prevention (ARISE-DP) program; Associate Clinical Core Leader, NYU Alzheimer Disease Research Center (NYU ADRC); Core Investigator, NYU Institute of Excellence in Health Equity; and a physician scientist with expertise in sleep, aging, and dementia research. My training reveals a commitment to an interdisciplinary, multilevel approach aiming to understand cognitive health in elderly populations. My medical and residency training provided me with knowledge of basic pathophysiological and biochemical mechanisms of neurological diseases affecting cognition. My Masters’ in Public Health provided me with research experiences of being under the tutelage of renowned scientists at Emory and the US Centers for Disease Prevention and Control (CDC), where I began to establish a line of empirical inquiry to understand health behaviors as risk factors for AD. My PhD training in neuro-epidemiology, at University of South Florida (USF) and postdoctoral fellowship training at NYU Grossman School of Medicine in the Department of Population Health aided in developing my scientific aptitude in sleep, cardiometabolic risk and cognition in older adults.  I have collaborated with experts in the field, first as student, a trainee, co-investigator and now recipient on intramural, foundation and NIH funded grants. Currently I have three active NIH funding including NIA K23AG068534, R01AG082278 and RF1AG083975; and 2 foundational awards including BrightFocus funded ADR #A2022033S and an Alzheimer’s Association funded AARG-D-21-848397. My NIA K23 examines the mediating role of slow wave sleep and vascular risk factors on Alzheimer Disease related disparity between African Americans and non-Hispanic Whites. The R01 is using a health disparity research framework to examine mechanisms linking obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) with higher Alzheimer’s disease (AD) risk in older Blacks/African Americans. The RF1 examines the treatment of OSA on sleep dependent memory and blood-based biomarkers in Blacks. The BrightFocus award is examining mechanisms of racial differences in the relationship between Obstructive Sleep Apnea and in vivo tau deposition in the context of amyloid burden. The Alzheimer’s Association grant is investigating race specific regional tau deposition and role of obstructive sleep apnea.  These awards have led to findings with significant contributions that serve as a critical step in the continuum of research expected to consolidate our understanding of how sleep disorders, changes in rest-activity patterns, and sleep-wake cycles can be utilized as predictive and preventive tools to identify and treat individuals with AD. One line of research that has been added to the literature consolidated the evidence of the association between sleep and AD and/or cognitive decline suggesting that approximately 15% of AD may be prevented should interventions be implemented to reduce sleep problems and disorders. We have also demonstrated that OSA severity is associated with the rate-of-change in AD pathology including amyloid and tau burden longitudinally across the spectrum of dementia. We also show that OSA acts in synergism with Aβ and with tau, and all three acting together result in synergistic neurodegenerative mechanisms especially as Aβ and tau accumulation becomes increasingly abnormal. My focus and logical extension to sleep health disparities and cognitive outcomes is also yielding important findings that will move the field of sleep and AD as it relates to minoritized populations.  Over the past 6 years, I have led the community-engaged research efforts in various NIH funded grants of my mentors and collaborators, and currently direct the NYU Sleep Disparity Workgroup that has built capacity to address sleep disparities in NYC, by engaging the community via non-traditional venues and practice-based settings. name: Jasmin Divers
title: Professor, Director
summary: Dr. Divers is Professor of Biostatistics and Director of the Division of Health Services Research in the Department of Foundations of Medicine at the NYU Grossman Long Island School of Medicine. He is a biostatistician with expertise in statistical genetics. Dr. Divers has a broad research portfolio that includes chronic kidney disease and end-stage kidney disease projects, kidney transplant outcomes, type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes mellitus, cognitive function, cerebrovascular disease, and cardiovascular disease. This is a proposed randomized controlled trial (RCT) to evaluate strategies for safely tapering patients of Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1) after achieving and maintaining the weight loss goals. Obesity affects over 40% of U.S. adults and significantly increases the risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. GLP-1’s have been shown to be effective at producing significant weight loss among overweight or obese patients. However, over 50% of patients initiated on a GLP-1 discontinuity the therapy within one year. It is unknown whether nutrition counseling and medically tailored meals can help maintain weight loss for overweight or obese patients during GLP-1 tapering or discontinuation. Before a definitive trial can be launched, it is essential to determine whether combining nutrition counseling and MTMs with a GLP-1 taper is feasible, acceptable, and operationally implementable in a real-world clinical setting. The SUSTAIN-RD (SUSTAINing GLP-1 Benefits with Medically Tailored Meals and registered dietitian counseling support is designed to address these questions. This randomized controlled pilot trial will evaluate the feasibility and acceptability of a multilayered nutrition intervention during and after GLP-1 tapering, to inform a future large-scale trial. The trial’s specific aims are to: (1) determine the feasibility of the SUSTAIN-RD pilot trial by assessing the following metrics: 1) eligibility, consent, enrollment rate, and retention rates; and (2) evaluate the acceptability of the SUSTAIN-RD trial, using quantitative and qualitative measures; and (3) generate preliminary estimates of weight and cardiometabolic changes to guide effect-size and power calculations for a future RCT. This pilot will provide data to inform a future full-scale trial to rigorously evaluate the efficacy and implementation of the intervention, including recruitment and enrollment strategies, trial design (e.g., sample size), and outcomes. I have served as PI or MPI on multiple NIH grants and Cooperative Agreements, including CASSIDY (1U18DK145716) and DiCAYA (U18DP006510). I am currently serving as the Data Coordinating Center lead for the TwoPlus trial (CER-2022C1-26300), a PCORI-funded multicenter trial to evaluate the comparative effectiveness of an individualized model of hemodialysis vs conventional hemodialysis. Dr. Divers will lead analytical efforts for the proposed trial and work in collaboration with Dr. Zhang and Dr. Lau. We have assembled an outstanding multidisciplinary team to work on this project. I have considerable expertise in all areas needed to address these questions, including study design, biostatistics, clinical trial management, and data analysis. Dr. Divers has the skills, experience, leadership, and motivation necessary to lead this proposal successfully

name: Olugbenga Ogedegbe
title: Professor & Director
summary: As a primary care physician at NYU Langone, I am dedicated to improving health outcomes for patients with chronic diseases with a particular focus on care of patients with hypertension and other cardiovascular disease risk factors. My approach to patient care is deeply rooted in understanding the social determinants of health, particularly within low-income and minoritized communities. I believe in a holistic approach to healthcare, ensuring that patients receive comprehensive and compassionate care tailored to their unique needs.  I specialize in managing patients with cardiovascular diseases, with a focus on reducing health inequities in diverse populations. My expertise extends to implementing clinic–community linkage models, which have proven effective in addressing disparities in health outcomes. Additionally, I am involved in global health initiatives, particularly in Africa, where my team has pioneered task-shifting strategies for high blood pressure control and other cardiovascular disease risk factors like diabetes.  With more than two decades of experience in clinical epidemiology, health services research, and implementation science, I bring a wealth of knowledge and innovative methodologies to my practice. My research has been extensively funded by the National Institutes of Health, and I have led numerous studies aimed at translating evidence-based interventions into clinical practice. My work here in the United States as well as in Ghana and Nigeria has been instrumental in shaping national health policies and improving high blood pressure management in these regions.  My educational journey began with a medical degree, followed by a master of public health and master of science in clinical epidemiology. My passion for medicine was ignited by a desire to address health disparities and improve access to quality care for diverse and adversely affected populations. As the founding director of the Institute for Excellence in Health Equity at NYU Langone, I am committed to integrating health equity solutions across our health system, ensuring that every patient receives the highest standard of care.

specialty_areas: African American patients / Black adults / Black churches / Black patients / Hispanic patients

publications.title: Comparative Effectiveness of a Practice-Based Comprehensive Lifestyle Intervention vs Single-Session Counseling in Hypertensive Blacks
publications.authors: Schoenthaler A; Luerassi L; Silver S; Odedosu T; Kong J; Ravenell J; Teresi JA; Ogedegbe G
publications.plain_language_summary: In a Bellevue primary-care population of Black adults with uncontrolled hypertension, a pragmatic single-session lifestyle counseling intervention achieved blood pressure reductions comparable to a more intensive multicomponent group program plus motivational interviewing over 6 months, highlighting feasibility and implementation tradeoffs for high-burden settings.
key_findings: Both arms showed substantial BP reductions at 6 months; no statistically significant between-group difference in SBP or DBP.
Attendance in the intensive group program was modest, consistent with structural barriers in low-income populations.
publications.equity_relevance: Focuses on pragmatic, scalable lifestyle approaches for BP control in populations disproportionately affected by hypertension morbidity and mortality.

publications.title: Home Blood Pressure Telemonitoring and Nurse Case Management in Black and Hispanic Patients With Stroke: A Randomized Clinical Trial
publications.authors: Ogedegbe G; Teresi JA; Williams SK; Ogunlade A; Izeogu C; Eimicke JP; Kong J; Silver SA; Williams O; Valsamis H; Law S; Levine SR; Waddy SP; Spruill TM
publications.plain_language_summary: Among predominantly low-income Black and Hispanic stroke survivors with uncontrolled hypertension across New York City health systems, adding telephonic nurse case management to home BP telemonitoring produced a substantially larger SBP reduction at 12 months than telemonitoring alone, supporting NCM-enhanced telehealth programs for equitable secondary prevention.
key_findings: SBP reduction at 12 months was greater with NCM plus telemonitoring than telemonitoring alone (between-group difference about 8 mm Hg).
Recurrent stroke rates at 24 months were similar between arms, likely due to low event counts.
publications.equity_relevance: Targets post-stroke BP control disparities using scalable, health-system integrated telehealth support for low-income Black and Hispanic populations.

publications.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Positive-Affect Intervention and Medication Adherence in Hypertensive African Americans
publications.authors: Ogedegbe GO; Boutin-Foster C; Wells MT; Allegrante JP; Isen AM; Jobe JB; Charlson ME
publications.plain_language_summary: A patient education adherence program augmented with positive-affect and self-affirmation techniques modestly improved objectively measured antihypertensive medication adherence over 12 months among African American patients, demonstrating a low-burden behavioral lever for equity-oriented BP control.
key_findings: Electronic pill-monitor adherence at 12 months was higher in the positive-affect group than education alone (42% vs 36%).
BP reductions were small and not significantly different between arms.
publications.equity_relevance: Uses culturally-tailored, scalable behavioral strategies to address adherence barriers contributing to hypertension disparities.

publications.title: Cluster Randomized Clinical Trial of FAITH (Faith-Based Approaches in the Treatment of Hypertension) in Blacks: Main Trial Results
publications.authors: Schoenthaler AM; Lancaster KJ; Chaplin W; Butler M; Forsyth J; Ogedegbe G
publications.plain_language_summary: Across 32 New York City churches, a lay health advisor–delivered lifestyle program plus motivational interviewing achieved greater SBP reduction than an attention-control health education program at 6 months, illustrating how trusted community institutions can deliver scalable hypertension control interventions.
key_findings: MINT-TLC produced a greater SBP reduction than health education at 6 months (about 5–6 mm Hg).
Effects were sustained but attenuated at 9 months; differences in DBP were not significant.
publications.equity_relevance: Demonstrates community-based delivery through Black churches, a culturally salient channel to reduce hypertension disparities.

publications.title: Counseling African Americans to Control Hypertension (CAATCH): Cluster Randomized Clinical Trial Main Effects
publications.authors: Ogedegbe G; Tobin JN; Fernandez S; Cassells A; Diaz-Gloster M; Khalida C; Pickering T; Schwartz JE
publications.plain_language_summary: In 30 community health centers serving predominantly low-income Black patients with uncontrolled hypertension, a multilevel intervention targeting both patients and clinicians did not significantly improve overall BP control compared with enhanced usual care, emphasizing the challenge of complex interventions and the need for efficient, tailored implementation strategies.
key_findings: BP control at 12 months was similar between intervention and enhanced usual care overall.
Prespecified subgroup analyses suggested better BP control in participants without diabetes and in smaller community health centers.
publications.equity_relevance: Large practice-based implementation trial in low-resource settings, directly addressing structural barriers and feasibility of multicomponent hypertension disparity interventions.

name: Stephen Wall
title: Professor
summary: The overall objective of this study is to identify early digital markers of glycemic and metabolic dysregulation in 200 trauma survivors using wearable technology and machine learning (ML) algorithms. As a Tenured Professor in the Departments of Emergency Medicine and Surgery at the NYU Grossman School of Medicine and Bellevue Hospital Center, I have the expertise, leadership, training, and motivation necessary to serve as a Co-Investigator for the proposed research. I have a broad background in health services outcomes and educational research methods. I have specific training and expertise in mixed methods, ethnographic, qualitative, and survey research and data analysis techniques including econometric modeling, program evaluation, and quantitative analysis of qualitative data. My research interests are in improving health behaviors of underserved populations using digital educational media and using modern surveillance techniques to identify problem areas amenable to intervention. As a Co-investigator on our collaborative New York State Funded traffic injury surveillance grants, I served as the methodologist and emergency medicine liaison supervising the data analysis and monitoring collaborations among emergency department providers to ensure recruitment targets were met. I also served as a site Principal Investigator for Identifying Road Hazards for Bicyclists, a study funded by the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety. Our work has led to significant contributions to the pedestrian and bicyclist injury literature with this project building upon our prior success. I also am contact-PI for a mixed methods project that is attempting to recover lungs from patients who die unexpectedly in emergency departments (R33HL156890, PIs Wall, Montgomery). Specific to this project, I have an established collaborative relationship with Dr. Schultebraucks who is serving as PI for this proposal. Currently, I serve as a Co-Investigator on multiple initiatives focusing on the importance of well-being and mental health in healthcare workers and patients experiencing various levels of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). Dr. Schultebraucks and I collaborate on NIH-funded R01HL156134, which analyzes expressive flexibility in healthcare workers and the linkage of the improvement of emotion regulation and resilience. Additionally, we collaborate on R01MH129856, which looks at large language models that predicts PTSD risk in patients, as many trauma survivors are impacted by PTSD post discharge. This work highlights the importance of using the ED as a vital point for early risk stratification, and carries well into the proposed research, building off of the infrastructure of our NIH-funded study of trauma survivors admitted to the ED after a potential traumatic event (R01MH129856). The addition of wearables and standard glycemic assessments will only further support the ED as a point of early detection of trauma, and mitigation of adverse metabolic outcomes in the long term.

As a Co-investigator on our collaborative New York State Funded traffic injury surveillance grants, I served as the methodologist and emergency medicine liaison supervising the data analysis and monitoring collaborations among emergency department providers to ensure recruitment targets were met. I also served as a site Principal Investigator for Identifying Road Hazards for Bicyclists, a study funded by the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety. Our work has led to significant contributions to the pedestrian and bicyclist injury literature with this project building upon our prior success. I also am contact-PI for a mixed methods project that is attempting to recover lungs from patients who die unexpectedly in emergency departments (R33HL156890, PIs Wall, Montgomery). Specific to this project, I have an established collaborative relationship with Dr. Schultebraucks who is serving as PI for this proposal. Currently, I serve as a Co-Investigator on multiple initiatives focusing on the importance of well-being and mental health in healthcare workers and patients experiencing various levels of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). Dr. Schultebraucks and I collaborate on NIH-funded R01HL156134, which analyzes expressive flexibility in healthcare workers and the linkage of the improvement of emotion regulation and resilience. Additionally, we collaborate on R01MH129856, which looks at large language models that predicts PTSD risk in patients, as many trauma survivors are impacted by PTSD post discharge. This work highlights the importance of using the ED as a vital point for early risk stratification, and carries well into the proposed research, building off of the infrastructure of our NIH-funded study of trauma survivors admitted to the ED after a potential traumatic event (R01MH129856). The addition of wearables and standard glycemic assessments will only further support the ED as a point of early detection of trauma, and mitigation of adverse metabolic outcomes in the long term.
summary_short: The overall objective of this study is to identify early digital markers of glycemic and metabolic dysregulation in 200 trauma survivors using wearable technology and machine learning (ML) algorithms. As a Tenured Professor in the Departments of Emergency Medicine and Surgery at the NYU Grossman School of Medicine and Bellevue Hospital Center, I have the expertise, leadership, training, and motivation necessary to serve as a Co-Investigator for the proposed research.

name: Natasha J. Williams
title: Associate Professor
summary: I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Population Health and Institute for Excellence in Health Equity. I received my Master of Social Work (MSW) from the University of Michigan, Master of Public Health (MPH) from Columbia University, Mailman School of Public Health, and my doctorate in health education and behavioral studies (EdD) from Teachers College, Columbia University. My work is grounded in community-based translational research in minoritized populations and demonstrates a long-term commitment to patient-oriented research to advance health equity. I have published in top peer reviewed journals including, Chest, Sleep Health, JAMA Open Network, American Journal of Public Health, and others. I have presented at dozens of scientific conferences and been featured in media outlets and magazines.  In 2020, I served as Multiple Principal Investigator (MPI) of two NIH Rapid Acceleration and Diagnostics for Underserved Populations (RADx-UP) studies to address disparities related to COVID-19. In addition, I was a member of the NIH Community Engagement Alliance (CEAL) Inclusive Participation Work Group. Since 2021 I have served as the Contact PI for the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI), funded Maternal Health Community Implementation Project (MHCIP). MHCIP engages communities to implement evidence-based interventions in addressing poor maternal outcomes such as hypertension, obesity and gestational diabetes in minoritized populations. The program at NYULH called Just Mothers is currently being implemented at NYC Health + Hospitals, the largest municipal health system in the country, and at the Family Health Centers at NYULH (part of a national network of Federally Qualified Health Centers) in the Sunset Park and Flatbush neighborhoods in Brooklyn. My approach to community engagement with diverse partners has been widely recognized for its potential impact in advancing health equity.

specialty_areas: COVID-19 disparities / COVID-19 outcomes / LMIC health equity / RADx-UP / RECOVER initiative

publications.title: County Incarceration Rate and Stroke Death: A Cross-Sectional Study of the Influence of Physical Environment, Health Care Access, and Community Mental Distress
publications.authors: Sonderlund AL; Wang EA; Williams NJ; Horowitz CR; Schoenthaler A; Holaday LW
publications.journal: Journal of the American Heart Association
publications.plain_language_summary: County imprisonment rates were positively associated with stroke mortality across 2260 US counties. The association was partly explained by worse food environments, lower primary care physician access, and higher community mental distress, consistent with incarceration operating as a structural determinant of cardiovascular outcomes and racial disparities.
key_findings: Imprisonment rate was associated with higher stroke death after adjustment (reported effect 0.08 with 95% CI 0.05–0.10).
Mediation evidence supported three pathways: food environment, primary care access, and community mental distress.
Counties with higher imprisonment rates and higher stroke death had substantially larger Black populations than low-quintile counties.
publications.equity_relevance: Positions incarceration as a measurable, place-based manifestation of structural racism with downstream health consequences and actionable mediators.

publications.title: Rapid Community Engagement in Response to SARS-CoV-2 Funding Opportunities: New York City, 2020–2021
publications.authors: Williams NJ; Gill E; Punter MA; Reiss J; Goodman M; Shelley D; Thorpe LE
publications.plain_language_summary: Describes how a New York City collaboration rapidly convened a Community Steering Committee with public housing residents and community organizations to respond to RADx-UP funding and testing inequities, and reports high partner satisfaction on validated engagement metrics.
key_findings: Used the validated REST instrument at 6 months; mean scores were high for both quality and quantity of engagement (both reported as 4.3).
Operationalized engagement principles including co-leadership by community members, transparency of data, and compensation for participation.
Documented practical sustainability lessons for maintaining engagement during fast-moving public health emergencies.
publications.equity_relevance: Provides a replicable model for rapid, compensated, resident-informed engagement in underserved communities during emergencies.

publications.title: Prevalence and factors associated with second hand smoke exposure among a sample of pregnant women in Cairo, Egypt
publications.authors: Stevens ER; Mead-Morse EL; Labib K; Kahn LG; Choi S; Sherman SE; Oncken C; Williams NJ; Loney T; El Shahawy O
publications.plain_language_summary: Surveyed 200 third-trimester pregnant women in Cairo clinics and found high secondhand smoke exposure, largely driven by husbands smoking in the home, alongside misconceptions about fetal harms, supporting gender-sensitive messaging and stronger enforcement of smoke-free policies.
key_findings: About two-thirds of participants had a husband who smoked (69%).
Among non-smokers, 38% reported secondhand smoke exposure during pregnancy.
Husband smoking in the home was strongly associated with higher daily exposure hours (p<0.001).
publications.equity_relevance: Links household power dynamics and policy enforcement gaps to preventable maternal and child health risks.

publications.title: A Roadmap for Development of Community Engagement: Early Lessons Learned from the RECOVER Initiative
publications.authors: Taylor BD; Albert SL; Marti HK; ...; Williams NJ; and the RECOVER Initiative
publications.journal: Progress in Community Health Partnerships: Research, Education, and Action
publications.citation: Progress in Community Health Partnerships. Advance online publication (Nov 2, 2025)
publications.plain_language_summary: Outlines early RECOVER engagement structures, decision-making principles, and implementation lessons to support sustained, equitable participation across a national research initiative.
key_findings: Provides an operational roadmap for community engagement structures and workflows within RECOVER.
Emphasizes principles such as shared governance, transparency, and ongoing bidirectional communication.
publications.equity_relevance: Offers concrete engagement infrastructure that centers trust and participation in large, multi-site research.

publications.title: Social Determinants of Health and Risk for Long COVID in the U.S. RECOVER-Adult Cohort
publications.authors: Feldman CH; Santacroce L; ...; Williams NJ; et al.
publications.plain_language_summary: In the RECOVER-Adult cohort, multiple pre-infection and peri-infection social risk factors were independently associated with higher risk of long COVID, highlighting targets for screening and intervention alongside biological risk stratification.
key_findings: Identifies social risk factors that are independently associated with long COVID risk in a large US cohort.
Supports incorporating SDoH measures into long COVID prediction, outreach, and care models.
publications.equity_relevance: Connects social risk to post-acute sequelae, providing evidence for equity-focused prevention and care delivery.

name: Collin J. Popp
title: Assistant Professor
summary: My mission as a scientist is to conduct interdisciplinary lifestyle interventions targeting obesity. In particular, my future research niche bridges the gap between biological and behavioral determinants associated with weight loss maintenance. As my long-term career goal, I aim to test novel strategies (e.g., time-restricted eating) to attenuate weight regain following successful weight loss. My doctoral training and research experience as a postdoctoral fellow have provided me with a broad background in exercise physiology and nutritional science, with specific training in the assessment of body composition (bioelectrical impedance analysis and dualenergy x-ray absorptiometry), exercise testing (Wingate anaerobic tests, graded exercise treadmill), energy expenditure (resting energy expenditure) and physical activity using free-living wearable devices (Sensewear, Actigraph). As a postdoctoral fellow, I expanded my research and interdisciplinary efforts to focus on adults with obesity and diabetes. Specifically, I served as a fellow on the American Heart Association-funded Personal Diet study, a bio-behavioral weight loss intervention in individuals with prediabetes and early-stage type 2 diabetes. I served as a co-investigator (PI: Sevick [retired]) and, currently, as the principal investigator on an NINR-funded award examining the impact of a precision nutrition intervention for the treatment of type 2 diabetes (R01NR018916). I was awarded a K99/R00 Pathway to Independence Career Development Award (K99HL163474) to examine the efficacy of timerestricted eating, a lifestyle intervention, for weight-loss maintenance in adults with obesity who have recently lost weight. Relevant to this application, I have extensive expertise in lifestyle interventions targeting weight loss, with experience measuring self-monitoring behaviors. I am well-suited to serve as a co-Investigator on this application entitled, 'Intensive behavioral support to improve adherence and outcomes for patients taking incretin mimetics for obesity."

name: Valy Fontil
title: Assistant Professor & Medical Director
summary: I am a practicing primary care physician, Medical Director of Research for the Family Health Centers at NYU Langone Health, and Associate Director of the NYU Program on Digital Health. My research focuses on designing, implementing, and rigorously evaluating technology-enabled, workflow-integrated interventions to improve management of common chronic conditions in primary care—particularly in safety-net settings where mental health care is predominantly delivered by primary care clinicians. I am a trained implementation scientist with extensive experience leading pragmatic, multi-site trials that target clinical decision-making, treatment intensification, and follow-up processes using EHR-embedded tools, audit-and-feedback dashboards, and measurement-based workflows. Across NIH- and PCORI-funded studies, I have developed and evaluated scalable interventions that translate evidencebased guidelines into routine care through provider-facing clinical decision support and population-based management tools. This includes leading large cluster-randomized and quasi-experimental studies in which the primary analytic focus is at the provider level, while accounting for clinic-level implementation and workflow constraints. My work has emphasized identifying and addressing gaps between routine measurement and timely treatment adjustment—an underlying mechanism that contributes to poor outcomes across multiple chronic conditions, including mental health. The proposed R61/R33 project directly builds on this body of work by extending measurement-based, technology-enabled approaches to the management of depression and anxiety in primary care. My experience integrating symptom monitoring, treatment algorithms, and peer-comparison feedback into existing clinical workflows positions me to lead the development, optimization, and evaluation of the proposed intervention and its associated implementation strategy. I am wellsuited to oversee this hybrid effectiveness-implementation study and to ensure that findings are both methodologically rigorous and directly actionable for health systems.

publications.title: Building a Learning Health System at a Federally Qualified Health Center to Advance Research For Health Equity, 2021–2024
publications.authors: Gore R; Dapkins IP; Fontil V
publications.plain_language_summary: Describes how a federally qualified health center built learning health system infrastructure to integrate research and quality improvement with routine care, enabling equity focused studies within safety net primary care.
key_findings: Details pragmatic steps to establish governance, data access, and operational workflows that support rapid cycle research in an FQHC setting.
Highlights barriers and enabling conditions for embedding research in safety net clinical environments.
Provides a replicable playbook for health systems aiming to align research capacity with equity goals.
publications.equity_relevance: Centers safety net settings and diverse patient populations, emphasizing infrastructure that reduces structural barriers to participation and supports equity oriented evidence generation.

publications.title: Improvement in Blood Pressure Control in Safety Net Clinics Receiving 2 Versions of a Scalable Quality Improvement Intervention: BP MAP A Pragmatic Cluster Randomized Trial
publications.authors: Fontil V; Faulkner Modrow M; Cooper-DeHoff RM; et al
publications.journal: Journal of the American Heart Association
publications.source_file: Vally_Fontil - Improvement in Blood Pressure Control.pdf
publications.plain_language_summary: A pragmatic multi health system trial tested two scalable versions of a BP quality improvement program in safety net clinics and measured changes in BP control and related care processes using EHR data.
key_findings: Both Full Support and Self Guided implementations led to modest, not statistically significant improvements in BP control at 6 months compared with usual care.
Confirmatory repeated BP measurement improved substantially in intervention clinics, but medication intensification did not improve, highlighting persistent therapeutic inertia.
Results point to the need for more hands on strategies such as decision support and workflow automation to drive treatment escalation and follow up.
publications.equity_relevance: Focuses on safety net clinics and scalable implementation approaches that can be deployed in under resourced settings where disparities in BP outcomes are common.

publications.title: Effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of team-based care for hypertension: a meta-analysis and simulation study
publications.authors: Bryant KB; Rao AS; Cohen LP; et al; Fontil V; Bellows BK
publications.source_file: Vally_Fontil - cost effectiveness of team-based care.pdf
publications.plain_language_summary: Combines trial evidence with a validated policy simulation model to estimate long term BP, cardiovascular events, and value for different team based hypertension care designs.
key_findings: Team based care with a non physician who can titrate medications yielded larger SBP reductions than physician titration in pooled trials.
Modeling suggested non physician titration strategies are highly cost effective relative to usual care over 10 years.
Results support reimbursement and workforce structures that enable pharmacists or other non physician clinicians to manage medications under protocols.
publications.equity_relevance: Provides evidence and economic rationale for scalable team based models that can improve BP control in communities facing access constraints and clinician shortages.

publications.title: Adaptation and Feasibility Study of a Digital Health Program to Prevent Diabetes among Low-Income Patients: Results from a Partnership between a Digital Health Company and an Academic Research Team
publications.authors: Fontil V; McDermott K; Tieu L; et al
publications.plain_language_summary: Evaluates adaptation and feasibility of a digital diabetes prevention program for low income patients through a partnership between a digital health company and an academic safety net team.
key_findings: Demonstrated feasibility of enrolling and engaging low income patients in a digital DPP style intervention when workflows and supports were tailored to barriers.
Identified implementation needs including onboarding support, access considerations, and integration with primary care.
Provided early evidence to guide scale up of digital prevention approaches in safety net settings.
publications.equity_relevance: Directly addresses digital access barriers and adaptation requirements for deploying prevention interventions in underserved populations.

publications.title: Simulating Strategies for Improving Control of Hypertension Among Patients with Usual Source of Care in the United States: The Blood Pressure Control Model
publications.authors: Fontil V; Bibbins-Domingo K; Kazi DS; et al
publications.plain_language_summary: A microsimulation model compared high leverage care processes for BP control, showing that improving treatment intensification and reliable follow up can drive much larger gains than adherence alone.
key_findings: In the model, enhancing treatment intensification produced the largest improvements in BP control compared with increasing visit frequency or adherence in isolation.
Combining idealized visit frequency, intensification, and adherence predicted near universal BP control within one year.
Provides quantitative guidance for prioritizing workflow and decision support interventions that reduce clinical inertia.
publications.equity_relevance: Identifies system levers that can be targeted in safety net settings where follow up reliability and clinical inertia contribute to disparities in chronic disease outcomes.

name: Megan Morris
title: Associate Professor
summary: The goal of my research is to improve the quality of health and healthcare services for people with disabilities. Specifically, I seek to understand and address both structural and interpersonal ableism that affects the care received by this population. To achieve this, I have developed a comprehensive research program focused on: (a) creating and implementing systematic methods for documenting patients’ disability status and accommodation needs in the Electronic Health Record; (b) improving communication and interactions between healthcare teams and people with disabilities through educational and clinical interventions; and (c) evaluating the implementation and effects of disability healthcare policies on provision of accessible healthcare. I have served as a PI on multiple funded studies to support these initiatives. My research and advocacy are deeply informed by both my personal and professional experiences with ableism in the healthcare setting. To facilitate the translation of research to practice and policy, I founded and currently direct the Disability Equity Collaborative, a community dedicated to advancing equitable care for people with disabilities. A key component of this collaborative is our learning collaborative with membership of over 50 healthcare organizations who meet regularly and are committed to advancing disability equity within their own institutions.

name: Robin Ortiz
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Dr. Ortiz is a physician-scientist that has been recognized as an international expert on the interface between relational health and intergenerational cardiovascular health. For example, she was awarded a prestigious Harold Amos Medical Faculty Program Award to study, “Exploring the Association Between the Caregiver-Child Relationship and Adult Cardiovascular Health: Implications for [reframing Adverse Childhood Experiences to] Positive Dyadic Experiences”. The premise of this work is that the defining characteristics of being a caregiver (positive dyadic experiences) can be harnessed to sustainably drive change in CVH at the level of the adult and lay the foundation for the CVH of their children. In her early career, she has already authored over 40 peer-reviewed manuscripts and was recently named a 40-Under-40 Leader in Health by the National Minority Quality Forum. Due to her unique experience and critical expertise, she has been selected to inform national policy related to relational health. She was one of the first scientific advisors to join the ACEs (Adverse Childhood Experiences) Aware Initiative through the Aurrera Health Group in partnership with The Office of the Surgeon General of California, Nadine Burke Harris. She has continued this work as she currently serves as a scientific advisor with particular emphasis on the area of health equity to the UCLA-UCSF ACEs Aware Family Resilience Network (UCAAN). One of her key roles is in sitting on a task force of experts convened to inform an algorithm in response to the identification and treatment of toxic stress. She most recently was invited to sit on the New York State Trauma Informed Resource Network (TIRN) Advisory Council, serving an important role at a time when New York state begins work to build relational health programming and policy including ACEs screening response.   Dr. Ortiz is an Assistant Professor of Pediatrics and Population Health and a faculty member in the Institute for Excellence in Health Equity at New York University Grossman School of Medicine. Dr. Ortiz is triple-board certified in internal medicine, pediatrics, and lifestyle medicine. She is the founder of the Child-Caregiver Heart Health Program. Her research program aims to 1) understand the mechanisms underpinning the association between stress physiology and cardiometabolic disease risk, and 2) identify and promote approaches to the attainment of optimized intergenerational cardiovascular health for disease prevention, with a focus on adults who are caregivers of children.   Throughout all of this work, she maintains clinical practice working with marginalized communities at Bellevue Hospital in the largest public hospital system in the country, New York City Health+Hospitals. She also serves as a true advocate and mentor of trainees across disciplines and educational programs.     Dr. Ortiz obtained her Bachelor of Science in behavioral neuroscience from Northeastern University, her medical degree from Loyola University Stritch School of Medicine in Chicago, and her residency training at the Johns Hopkins Hospital. Dr. Ortiz has also received research training at the National Institutes for Health in Bethesda, Maryland. Before joining NYU as faculty, she completed the prestigious National Clinician Scholars Program at the University of Pennsylvania and The Children’s Hospital of Philadelphia, where she simultaneously obtained a Master of Science degree in health policy research.

publications.title: A Community-Engaged Approach for Assessment of Cortisol Dynamic Range and Multilevel Chronic Stress in African Americans: FAITH! Heart Health+ Ancillary Study
publications.authors: Ortiz R; Joseph JJ; Johnson MP; Moen L; Lalika M; Jones C; Bancos I; Cooper LA; Hayes SN; Patten CA; Brewer LC
publications.plain_language_summary: In a community-engaged ancillary study of the FAITH! Heart Health+ cohort, participants self-collected morning and late-day saliva samples to estimate cortisol dynamic range as a biomarker of chronic stress. Collection was feasible, and among women, lower dynamic range was associated with higher perceived stress, anxiety, high-effort coping (Superwoman Schema), and a geospatial proxy related to structural racism.
key_findings: Participant-led saliva collection for two timepoints was feasible, with about 70% returning samples and most showing physiologic morning>evening cortisol patterns.
Among African American women, flatter cortisol dynamic range was associated with higher perceived stress and anxiety and higher Superwoman Schema scores.
Geospatial context variables, including distance from George Floyd Square, were associated with cortisol dynamic range in women in exploratory models.
publications.equity_relevance: Connects multilevel racism-related stress exposures to physiologic dysregulation using community-driven methods that can support mechanistic and intervention studies in marginalized communities.

publications.title: Evidence for the Association between Adverse Childhood Family Environment, Child Abuse, and Caregiver Warmth and Cardiovascular Health across the lifespan: The Coronary Artery Risk Development in Young Adults (CARDIA) Study
publications.authors: Ortiz R; Kershaw KN; Zhao S; Kline D; Brock G; Jaffee S; Golden SH; Ogedegbe O; Carroll J; Seeman TE; Joseph JJ
publications.source_file: Robin_Ortiz Evidence for the Association between Adverse.pdf
publications.plain_language_summary: Using CARDIA follow-up data, this study links a retrospectively measured risky family environment in childhood to worse longitudinal cardiovascular health across 20 years of adulthood. Child abuse was associated with lower odds of ideal cardiovascular health, while caregiver warmth was associated with higher odds, and adult income appeared to modify some associations.
key_findings: Higher risky family environment scores were associated with lower cardiovascular health scores over time and lower odds of ideal cardiovascular health across follow-up.
Child abuse and caregiver warmth subscales showed opposite associations with ideal cardiovascular health, with warmth linked to higher odds.
Associations between childhood environment and adult cardiovascular health differed by adult income strata in stratified analyses.
publications.equity_relevance: Frames childhood adversity and relational health as upstream drivers of cardiovascular disparities and supports intervention models that integrate family, economic opportunity, and primordial prevention.

publications.title: A Content Analysis of US Sanctuary Immigration Policies: Implications for Research in Social Determinants of Health
publications.authors: Ortiz R; Farrell-Bryan D; Gutierrez G; Boen C; Tam V; Yun K; Venkataramani AS; Montoya-Williams D
publications.source_file: Robin_Ortiz et al_Sanctuary Policy Content Analysis_Health Affairs_Final publication.pdf
publications.plain_language_summary: This paper catalogs and analyzes sanctuary policies enacted across the US from 2009 to 2017. It finds substantial heterogeneity in policy content, with many policies including health-related language about access to services, safety, and well-being, and with co-occurrence of inclusion and antidiscrimination themes that can guide future health equity evaluation.
key_findings: Between 2009 and 2017, hundreds of sanctuary policies were enacted across many states, with spikes in 2014 and 2017 and variation by jurisdiction level and policy type.
A large subset included explicit health-related or salutogenic language (health, access to services, rights, safety, well-being), often co-occurring with inclusion, trust, and privacy themes.
The results support stratifying sanctuary policies by content when studying associations with health outcomes.
publications.equity_relevance: Provides a structured policy taxonomy for studying how protective immigration policies shape access, fear, discrimination, and health outcomes.

publications.title: Childhood Environment, Early Life Stress, Caregiver Warmth, and Associations with Cortisol Diurnal Curve: The Coronary Artery Risk Development in Young Adults (CARDIA) Study
publications.authors: Ortiz R; Zhao S; Kline D; Brock G; Carroll J; Seeman TE; Jaffee S; Berger JS; Golden SH; Carnethon M; Joseph JJ
publications.source_file: Robin_Ortiz - Childhood Environment Early Life Stress, Caregiver Warmth,.pdf
publications.plain_language_summary: In CARDIA ancillary participants with intensive salivary cortisol sampling, higher early life stress was associated with higher wake-up cortisol and a flatter cortisol awakening response. Caregiver warmth showed the opposite association with the awakening response, and adult social support attenuated early-life stress associations in adjusted models.
key_findings: Greater risky family environment scores were associated with higher wake-up cortisol and a flatter cortisol awakening response after adjustment for age, sex, income, and smoking.
Higher caregiver warmth was associated with a steeper cortisol awakening response, while child abuse alone was not strongly associated with diurnal curve features.
Adult social support attenuated associations between early life stress and morning cortisol features, suggesting life-course relational context matters.
publications.equity_relevance: Links childhood adversity and relational health to physiologic stress regulation, providing mechanistic grounding for equity-focused interventions across the life course.

name: Joseph E. Ravenell
title: Associate Dean, Director, Associate Professor
summary: My professional career has been dedicated to achieving health equity for minoritized populations through clinical practice and community-based research. My research focuses on community-engaged approaches to improving hypertension-related outcomes in Black men.  I have been Principal Investigator of multiple NIH and CDC grant-funded projects to test community-based strategies focused on improving awareness and management of hypertension and improving cancer screening among black men throughout New York City.  This work led directly to a community-based research network of over 200 community-based sites including churches, barbershops, mosques and soup kitchens throughout New York City that serve Black and Latine individuals.   To date, my research has:  1) illuminated Black men’s perceptions of health and barriers to primary care use  2) established the barbershop as a site for service-oriented community-based intervention trials to address cardiovascular disease in high-risk populations  3) advanced translational research by testing clinically proven interventions in community-based settings to address disparities in hypertension and colorectal cancer  4) developed culturally-targeted behavioral interventions for hypertensive black patients in clinical settings  My work has expanded from effectiveness research to implementation research, exploring how evidence-based solutions to address hypertension I previously tested can be implemented in real-world settings and circumstances. I currently lead a project to test an intervention led by community health workers to facilitate hypertension prevention among 400 Black men in 22 Black-owned barbershops on the North Shore of Staten Island, New York

name: Britta Roy
title: Associate Professor,  Director, Pillar Lead
summary: Dr. Roy is a clinician-investigator who co-developed the Collective Well-being theoretical framework. Her scholarly work tests this framework using (1) epidemiology to identify psychosocial factors that equitably promote health and (2) community-based implementation science and systems science to design and test interventions to foster equitable health and well-being. Through her role in Beyond Bridges, she develops community-clinic-academic partnerships to improve population health and well-being among a diverse immigrant community in Brooklyn, NY. Dr. Roy also enjoys teaching and caring for patients as an academic hospitalist. Dr. Roy received a Bachelors in Engineering from Vanderbilt University and a Masters in Engineering from Wayne State University. She then completed a combined MD/MPH at the University of Michigan before completing residency and Chief Residency in internal medicine at the University of Alabama at Birmingham. Dr. Roy was a Robert Wood Johnson Foundation Clinical Scholar at Yale University from 2013-2015.

name: Safiya Richardson
title: Assistant Professor
summary: As an NYU Langone internal medicine physician, I provide comprehensive care for adults, which includes preventive and wellness visits and treatment for both acute and chronic complex medical conditions.  My approach is grounded in an integrated care model. I collaborate closely with specialists to ensure my patients receive the most up-to-date and evidence-based care.  In my practice, I focus on the benefits of lifestyle interventions, such as sleep, exercise, and nutrition, to address many common chronic illnesses. I aim to empower my patients to take control of their health and wellbeing, to ensure a comprehensive and holistic approach to their care. Together we can work toward long-term positive outcomes and improved quality of life.  When not caring for patients, I am involved in cutting-edge research focused on developing and implementing high-impact health information technology and clinical decision support tools. My dedication to improving healthcare delivery and patient care through digital health innovations has been recognized by Business Insider, which honored me as one of its 30 leaders under 40 transforming the healthcare landscape.

publications.title: Behavioral Economics and Medication Adherence for Hypertension: A Randomized Clinical Trial
publications.authors: Dodson JA; Adhikari S; Schoenthaler AM; Shimbo D; Berman AN; Levy N; Hanley K; Richardson S; Varghese A; Meng Y; et al; Troxel AB
publications.journal: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
publications.citation: J Am Coll Cardiol. 2025; article in press
publications.plain_language_summary: BETTER-BP tested an SMS-delivered behavioral economics regret-lottery to improve antihypertensive adherence in three New York City safety-net clinics. The lottery doubled adequate adherence over 6 months (71% vs 34%) but did not significantly improve systolic blood pressure compared with passive monitoring alone, and adherence gains were not sustained after incentives stopped.
key_findings: Adequate adherence (≥80% days adherent) was achieved by 71% in the lottery arm vs 34% in control at 6 months (adjusted risk ratio 2.04).
Systolic blood pressure declined similarly in both arms at 6 months (−6.7 vs −5.8 mm Hg; P=0.62).
After the lottery ended, adequate adherence fell and was similar between arms from 6–12 months (31% vs 26%).
publications.equity_relevance: Conducted in a diverse, largely Medicaid-insured or uninsured population, demonstrating feasibility of a technology-enabled adherence intervention in safety-net settings and clarifying the boundary between short-term adherence nudges and blood-pressure control.
